content:
# Disagreement Log — Leviticus 19

**Chapter:** Leviticus 19 (37 verses)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA
**Sources compared:** EMET (Literal) | LOGOS (Formal) | PNEUMA (Dynamic)

---

## Summary

Total disagreements logged: **62**

Leviticus 19 produced the highest disagreement count of any Holiness Code chapter to date, reflecting both the chapter's length (37 verses) and the extraordinary density of legally precise Hebrew terms that each agent rendered differently. The most significant category of disagreement is PNEUMA's systematic substitution of "foreigner" for ger (vv. 10, 33, 34) in violation of the project's binding terminology ruling. Other major areas of divergence include: pigul (v. 7); pe'ah/leqet rendering (vv. 9–10); the absolute-infinitive rebuke command (v. 17); yid'onim (v. 31); tsedeq in commercial measures (v. 36); and mishpatai (v. 37).

---

## Disagreement Log

**D-LEV19-001 | V. 1 | Name form**
- EMET: "Moses, saying" → "Moshe, saying"
- LOGOS: "Moses, saying"
- PNEUMA: "Moses"
- Ruling: "Moses" — English name form per project convention. EMET's "Moshe" rejected.

---

**D-LEV19-002 | V. 2 | "congregation" vs. "community"**
- EMET: "entire congregation of the sons of Israel"
- LOGOS: "entire congregation of the sons of Israel"
- PNEUMA: "whole community of Israel"
- Ruling: "entire congregation of the sons of Israel" — kol adat bney Yisra'el requires preservation of the formal legal term eda ("congregation") and the phrase "sons of Israel." PNEUMA's "community" loses the legal register and "of Israel" drops the bney construct.

---

**D-LEV19-003 | V. 2 | "for" vs. "because"**
- EMET/LOGOS: "for I — the LORD your God — am holy"
- PNEUMA: "because I — the LORD your God — am holy"
- Ruling: "for" — the Hebrew ki functions here as a grounding causal particle; "for" (EMET/LOGOS) is the traditional and more precise rendering of the theological ground of the command.

---

**D-LEV19-004 | V. 3 | "revere" vs. "honor"**
- EMET/LOGOS: "revere his mother and his father"
- PNEUMA: "honor his mother and his father"
- Ruling: "revere" — tira'u is from yare ("to fear, stand in awe of"), which is the stronger term and deliberately distinct from kaved ("honor," used in the Decalogue). PNEUMA's "honor" collapses the distinction.

---

**D-LEV19-005 | V. 3 | "Each man" vs. "Every one of you"**
- EMET: "Each man his mother and his father you shall revere"
- LOGOS/PNEUMA: "Every one of you shall revere his mother and his father"
- Ruling: "Every one of you shall revere his mother and his father" — LOGOS/PNEUMA's construction is more natural in English and correctly captures the plural-distributive sense of ish + plural verb. EMET's object-first word order reproduces Hebrew syntax but is awkward in English.

---

**D-LEV19-006 | V. 3 | Capitalization of "sabbaths"**
- EMET/PNEUMA: lowercase "sabbaths"
- LOGOS: capitalized "Sabbaths"
- Ruling: lowercase "sabbaths" — project convention.

---

**D-LEV19-007 | V. 4 | "the idols" vs. "idols" (article)**
- EMET: "Do not turn to the idols [ha-elilim]"
- LOGOS/PNEUMA: "Do not turn to idols"
- Ruling: "the idols" — the Hebrew ha-elilim has the definite article. Retaining it matches the MT and the polemical force of the phrase (these specific "non-entities").

---

**D-LEV19-008 | V. 4 | "molten gods" vs. "cast images of gods" vs. "gods of cast metal"**
- EMET: "molten gods [elohei massekah]"
- LOGOS: "cast images of gods"
- PNEUMA: "gods of cast metal for yourselves"
- Ruling: "molten gods" — elohei massekah is a compound: "gods of/by casting." EMET's "molten gods" most compactly renders the single Hebrew phrase without awkward separation of "gods" from the casting modifier.

---

**D-LEV19-009 | V. 5 | "slaughter" vs. "offer"**
- EMET: "slaughter a sacrifice of peace-offerings... you shall slaughter it"
- LOGOS: "offer a sacrifice of peace offerings... offer it"
- PNEUMA: "offer a peace offering... offer it"
- Ruling: "offer a sacrifice of peace offerings" — "offer" is the more natural English term for the broader sacrificial act; the word "sacrifice" already carries the slaughter-connotation.

---

**D-LEV19-010 | V. 5 | "for your acceptance" vs. paraphrases**
- EMET: "for your acceptance [liretzonkhem]"
- LOGOS: "so that you may be accepted"
- PNEUMA: "in a way that you can be accepted"
- Ruling: "for your acceptance" (EMET) — liretzonkhem is the technical term for a validly accepted offering; the compact prepositional phrase preserves the technical register. LOGOS/PNEUMA's clausal paraphrases are verbose.

---

**D-LEV19-011 | V. 6 | "day of your sacrifice" vs. variants**
- EMET: "On the day of your slaughtering"
- LOGOS: "on the day of your sacrifice"
- PNEUMA: "on the day you offer it"
- Ruling: "On the day of your sacrifice" (LOGOS) — balanced between EMET's literalism and PNEUMA's verbless clause.

---

**D-LEV19-012 | V. 6 | "on the next day" vs. "on the morrow"**
- EMET: "on the morrow"
- LOGOS/PNEUMA: "on the next day"
- Ruling: "on the next day" — "the morrow" is an archaism.

---

**D-LEV19-013 | V. 6 | "burned with fire" vs. variants**
- EMET: "in fire it shall be burned"
- LOGOS: "burned with fire"
- PNEUMA: "burned up"
- Ruling: "burned with fire" (LOGOS) — the Hebrew ba-esh yisaref ("in/with fire it shall be burned") is best rendered with LOGOS's natural English. PNEUMA's "burned up" adds a completive sense not in the text.

---

**D-LEV19-014 | V. 7 | pigul rendering**
- EMET: "a putrid thing [pigul]"
- LOGOS: "an offensive thing"
- PNEUMA: "tainted"
- Ruling: "an offensive thing [pigul]" — combining LOGOS's rendering with EMET's preserved technical term. "Putrid" (EMET) overemphasizes physical decay; "tainted" (PNEUMA) is too mild; "offensive thing" captures the ritual-sacral invalidity.

---

**D-LEV19-015 | V. 8 | "the one who eats" vs. "everyone who eats"**
- EMET: "the one who eats it shall bear his iniquity"
- LOGOS: "everyone who eats it shall bear his iniquity"
- PNEUMA: "Anyone who eats it will bear the guilt of that offense"
- Ruling: "everyone who eats it shall bear his iniquity" (LOGOS) — the participle with article functions as a distributive; PNEUMA's "guilt of that offense" and future tense "will bear" are paraphrastic.

---

**D-LEV19-016 | V. 8 | word order in profanation clause**
- EMET: "the holy thing of the LORD he has profaned"
- LOGOS: "he has profaned what is holy to the LORD"
- PNEUMA: "they have profaned what is holy to the LORD"
- Ruling: "he has profaned what is holy to the LORD" (LOGOS) — EMET's Hebrew word order is awkward in English; PNEUMA's plural "they" is inconsistent with the singular subject.

---

**D-LEV19-017 | V. 9 | pe'ah rendering**
- EMET: "you shall not complete the corner [pe'at] of your field in your reaping"
- LOGOS: "you shall not reap your field right to its edge"
- PNEUMA: "do not harvest all the way to the edge of your field"
- Ruling: "you shall not reap to the corner [pe'ah] of your field" — preserves the technical term with bracket; "complete the corner" (EMET) is awkward English; LOGOS/PNEUMA's "edge" without the technical term loses pe'ah.

---

**D-LEV19-018 | V. 9 | leqet rendering**
- EMET: "the gleaning [leqet] of your harvest you shall not gather"
- LOGOS: "the gleanings of your harvest you shall not gather" (no bracket)
- PNEUMA: "do not go back to pick up what the harvesters missed"
- Ruling: "the gleanings [leqet] of your harvest you shall not gather" — technical term preserved with bracket on first occurrence; PNEUMA's expansion ("go back to pick up what the harvesters missed") loses the technical term entirely.

---

**D-LEV19-019 | V. 10 | "strip bare" vs. "go over a second time"**
- EMET: "your vineyard you shall not go over a second time [lo te'olel]"
- LOGOS/PNEUMA: "you shall not strip your vineyard bare"
- Ruling: "you shall not strip your vineyard bare" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) — more natural English for lo te'olel.

---

**D-LEV19-020 | V. 10 | "sojourner" vs. "foreigner" — CRITICAL RULING VIOLATION**
- EMET/LOGOS: "for the poor and for the sojourner"
- PNEUMA: "for the poor and for the foreigner living among you"
- Ruling: "for the poor and for the sojourner" — binding project ruling. The Hebrew ger = "sojourner" throughout. PNEUMA's "foreigner" violates the chapter brief and project memory. Rejected.

---

**D-LEV19-021 | V. 11 | "deal falsely" vs. "deny falsely" vs. "deceive"**
- EMET: "you shall not deny falsely [lo tekachashу]"
- LOGOS: "you shall not deal falsely"
- PNEUMA: "Do not deceive one another"
- Ruling: "you shall not deal falsely" (LOGOS) — kachash in Pi'el denotes deceptive conduct; "deal falsely" is more precise than "deny falsely" (too specific) or "deceive one another" (merges with the third prohibition).

---

**D-LEV19-022 | V. 11 | "one to his neighbor" vs. "to one another"**
- EMET: "you shall not lie one to his neighbor"
- LOGOS/PNEUMA: "you shall not lie to one another"
- Ruling: "you shall not lie to one another" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) — ish le-amito is best rendered idiomatically as "to one another."

---

**D-LEV19-023 | V. 12 | consequential waw in profanation clause**
- EMET: "and you shall profane the name of your God"
- LOGOS: "and so profane the name of your God"
- PNEUMA: "that would be profaning your God's name"
- Ruling: "and so profane the name of your God" (LOGOS) — the waw-consecutive indicates consequence; "and so" captures it. EMET's rendering reads as a second independent command; PNEUMA's paraphrase loses the verbal force and restructures as a conditional.

---

**D-LEV19-024 | V. 13 | "hired man" vs. "hired worker"**
- EMET: "wages of a hired man [sekhar sakir]"
- LOGOS/PNEUMA: "wages of a hired worker"
- Ruling: "wages of a hired worker" (LOGOS) — "worker" is more inclusive and accurate for sakir; "man" over-specifies the gender.

---

**D-LEV19-025 | V. 13 | word order of wages clause**
- EMET: "the wages of a hired man shall not remain with you overnight until morning"
- LOGOS: "The wages of a hired worker shall not remain with you all night until the morning"
- PNEUMA: "Do not hold back a hired worker's wages overnight"
- Ruling: "the wages of a hired worker shall not remain with you overnight until morning" — LOGOS adds "all night" and "the morning" (slightly verbose); PNEUMA's "hold back" is a dynamic interpretation; EMET's structure without "all night" is cleaner.

---

**D-LEV19-026 | V. 14 | "in front of" addition**
- EMET/LOGOS: "before the blind you shall not put a stumbling block"
- PNEUMA: "do not put a stumbling block in front of the blind"
- Ruling: "before the blind you shall not put a stumbling block" (EMET/LOGOS) — "before" is the literal Hebrew lifnei and the traditional English rendering.

---

**D-LEV19-027 | V. 14 | "you shall fear" vs. "Fear"**
- EMET/LOGOS: "you shall fear your God"
- PNEUMA: "Fear your God"
- Ruling: "you shall fear your God" — the Hebrew ve-yare'ta is a second-person imperfect (command form); "you shall fear" represents the modal force correctly and maintains stylistic consistency.

---

**D-LEV19-028 | V. 15 | "be partial" vs. "show partiality" vs. "show favoritism"**
- EMET: "you shall not show partiality to the poor [lo tissa feni dal]"
- LOGOS: "you shall not be partial to the poor"
- PNEUMA: "Do not show favoritism to the poor"
- Ruling: "you shall not be partial to the poor" (LOGOS) — the most compact and precise.

---

**D-LEV19-029 | V. 15 | "honor the great" vs. "defer to the great" vs. "deference to the powerful"**
- EMET: "you shall not honor the great [lo tahdar pene gadol]"
- LOGOS: "nor defer to the great"
- PNEUMA: "deference to the powerful"
- Ruling: "you shall not defer to the great" (LOGOS) — tahdar (from hadar, "to honor sycophantically, to show deference") in this judicial context denotes deferential partiality; "defer" captures the judicial nuance better than "honor" (EMET) or "powerful" for gadol (PNEUMA's interpretation).

---

**D-LEV19-030 | V. 15 | "judge your neighbor" vs. "judge your neighbor with fairness"**
- EMET/LOGOS: "in righteousness you shall judge your neighbor"
- PNEUMA: "judge your neighbor with fairness"
- Ruling: "in righteousness you shall judge your neighbor" — the Hebrew be-tsedeq tishpot amitekha places tsedeq ("righteousness") in emphatic position before the verb; the consensus preserves this emphasis. PNEUMA's appended "with fairness" shifts both the emphasis and the vocabulary (tsedeq → fairness).

---

**D-LEV19-031 | V. 16 | "stand against the blood" vs. "stand by while life is at stake"**
- EMET/LOGOS: "you shall not stand against the blood of your neighbor"
- PNEUMA: "Do not stand by while your neighbor's life is at stake"
- Ruling: "you shall not stand against the blood of your neighbor" (EMET/LOGOS) — the Hebrew idiom lo ta'amod al dam re'akha is preserved without resolving the interpretive dispute between the false-testimony reading and the failure-to-rescue reading. PNEUMA's paraphrase chooses one reading and loses the blood idiom.

---

**D-LEV19-032 | V. 17 | "rebuke" vs. "reprove" vs. "confront"**
- EMET: "you shall surely rebuke [hokhei'ach tokhiach] your neighbor"
- LOGOS: "you shall surely reprove your neighbor"
- PNEUMA: "Confront your neighbor directly when he wrongs you"
- Ruling: "you shall surely rebuke your neighbor" (EMET) — the absolute infinitive construction is preserved by "shall surely"; "rebuke" is the more natural English for interpersonal correction in context; PNEUMA's expansion ("directly when he wrongs you") adds a triggering condition not in the Hebrew.

---

**D-LEV19-033 | V. 17 | "bear sin because of him" vs. "incur sin" vs. "share in his guilt"**
- EMET: "you shall not bear sin because of him"
- LOGOS: "lest you incur sin because of him"
- PNEUMA: "so that you do not share in his guilt"
- Ruling: "you shall not bear sin because of him" (EMET) — the Hebrew ve-lo tissa alav chet is "and you shall not bear sin upon/because of him." EMET's rendering is the most literal and uses the established project idiom "bear sin."

---

**D-LEV19-034 | V. 18 | "bear a grudge" vs. "hold a grudge"**
- EMET/LOGOS: "bear a grudge"
- PNEUMA: "hold a grudge"
- Ruling: "bear a grudge" (EMET/LOGOS) — the established idiom; also consistent with the Hebrew nasa ("to bear/carry").

---

**D-LEV19-035 | V. 19 | "cause to breed" vs. "let breed" vs. "breed two different kinds together"**
- EMET: "your cattle you shall not cause to mate with a different kind [kil'ayim]"
- LOGOS: "You shall not let your cattle breed with a different kind"
- PNEUMA: "Do not breed two different kinds of animals together"
- Ruling: "your cattle you shall not cause to breed with a different kind" — "cause to breed" is the Hiphil causative; "let breed" (LOGOS) is passive; PNEUMA's "two different kinds of animals together" loses the direct prohibition form and repeats "kinds."

---

**D-LEV19-036 | V. 19 | garment mixing law**
- EMET: "a garment of mixed-stuff [sha'atnez], two kinds, shall not come upon you"
- LOGOS: "nor shall you wear a garment made of two kinds of material mixed together"
- PNEUMA: "Do not put on clothing woven from two different materials"
- Ruling: "a garment of two kinds of mixed material shall not come upon you" — EMET's "mixed-stuff [sha'atnez]" preserves the technical term but is retained in the note rather than the text body; "shall not come upon you" preserves the unusual Hebrew idiom lo ya'aleh alekha (the garment "shall not come up upon you").

---

**D-LEV19-037 | V. 20 | "slave-woman" vs. "slave"**
- EMET/PNEUMA: "slave-woman"
- LOGOS: "slave" (gender-neutral)
- Ruling: "slave-woman" — shifchah is specifically a female slave; the gender is legally significant in this law.

---

**D-LEV19-038 | V. 20 | "pledged" vs. "designated"**
- EMET/PNEUMA: "pledged to a man"
- LOGOS: "designated for another man"
- Ruling: "pledged to a man" — necharefet (from chrf) implies betrothal/assignment; "pledged" captures the relational/covenantal dimension better than the more administrative "designated."

---

**D-LEV19-039 | V. 20 | "inquiry shall be held/made/there must be"**
- EMET: "an inquiry shall be made [biqoret tihyeh]"
- LOGOS: "an inquiry shall be held"
- PNEUMA: "there must be an inquiry"
- Ruling: "an inquiry shall be held" (LOGOS) — most natural English while preserving the impersonal-passive legal form.

---

**D-LEV19-040 | V. 20 | "redeeming she has not been redeemed"**
- EMET: "redeeming she has not been redeemed nor freedom given to her"
- LOGOS/PNEUMA: "has not been redeemed or given her freedom" / "not yet been ransomed or given her freedom"
- Ruling: "she has not been redeemed nor given her freedom" — smooth rendering without EMET's absolute-infinitive construction; "redeemed" preferred over PNEUMA's "ransomed."

---

**D-LEV19-041 | V. 23 | "treat as forbidden" vs. "count as forbidden"**
- EMET/PNEUMA: "treat its fruit as forbidden"
- LOGOS: "count its fruit as forbidden"
- Ruling: "treat its fruit as forbidden" (EMET/PNEUMA) — va-araltem is an active verb of action/treatment; "treat as" is more dynamic than "count as."

---

**D-LEV19-042 | V. 23 | circumcision gloss preservation**
- EMET: "three years it shall be treated as uncircumcised [arelim] for you"
- LOGOS: "Three years it shall be forbidden to you"
- PNEUMA: "For three years it is forbidden to you"
- Ruling: consensus retains the bracketed gloss "[va-araltem orlato — its foreskin, its uncircumcised fruit]" to preserve the circumcision metaphor that LOGOS and PNEUMA both omit.

---

**D-LEV19-043 | V. 24 | "praise-offering" vs. "offering of praise" vs. "holy offering of praise"**
- EMET: "a praise-offering [hillulim] to the LORD"
- LOGOS: "an offering of praise to the LORD"
- PNEUMA: "a holy offering of praise to the LORD"
- Ruling: "an offering of praise to the LORD" (LOGOS) — most natural English; PNEUMA's "holy" is not in the Hebrew for hillulim (holiness is expressed by qodesh in the same verse, applied to "all its fruit").

---

**D-LEV19-044 | V. 25 | "add its produce" vs. "increase its yield" vs. "your harvest will increase"**
- EMET: "for you to add its produce [le-hosif lakhem tevuato]"
- LOGOS: "to increase its yield for you"
- PNEUMA: "your harvest will increase"
- Ruling: "to increase its yield for you" (LOGOS) — the Hiphil of yasaf with tevuato ("its produce") is best rendered as "increase its yield." EMET's "add" is awkward; PNEUMA loses the reference to the tree's specific yield.

---

**D-LEV19-045 | V. 26 | "eat upon the blood" vs. "eat anything with the blood"**
- EMET: "You shall not eat upon the blood [al ha-dam]"
- LOGOS: "You shall not eat anything with the blood"
- PNEUMA: "Do not eat anything with the blood still in it"
- Ruling: "You shall not eat upon the blood" (EMET) — al ha-dam is the literal phrase; "upon the blood" preserves the ambiguity of al (proximity/manner) vs. content. LOGOS/PNEUMA both interpret the preposition as indicating content ("with the blood").

---

**D-LEV19-046 | V. 26 | divination terms**
- EMET: "not practice divination [lo tenachashу]... not practice augury [lo te'onenu]"
- LOGOS: "not interpret omens or tell fortunes"
- PNEUMA: "not practice divination or seek omens"
- Ruling: "You shall not practice divination and you shall not practice augury" (EMET) — maintains the two distinct technical prohibitions as parallel clauses. "Augury" for te'onenu (cloud-reading/auspicious-timing observation) is more precise than LOGOS's "tell fortunes" or PNEUMA's "seek omens."

---

**D-LEV19-047 | V. 27 | head-corner prohibition**
- EMET: "You shall not round off the corner [pe'at] of your head"
- LOGOS: "You shall not round off the hair on your temples"
- PNEUMA: "Do not cut the hair at the sides of your head"
- Ruling: "You shall not round off the corner [pe'ah] of your head" — "round off" (EMET/LOGOS) is the most accurate for naqaf (Hiphil, "to go around/encircle in cutting"); "corner/side of the head" preserved with pe'ah bracket; LOGOS's "hair on your temples" and PNEUMA's "sides of your head" interpret the location without preserving the term.

---

**D-LEV19-048 | V. 27 | beard-corner prohibition**
- EMET: "you shall not destroy the corner [pe'at] of your beard"
- LOGOS: "you shall not mar the edges of your beard"
- PNEUMA: "clip the edges of your beard"
- Ruling: "you shall not mar the corner [pe'ah] of your beard" — "mar" (LOGOS) for tashchit is the established rendering that avoids the over-strong "destroy" (EMET) and the too-mild "clip" (PNEUMA). Bracket retained for pe'ah consistency.

---

**D-LEV19-049 | V. 28 | "cutting for the dead"**
- EMET: "a cutting for the dead [seret la-nefesh] you shall not make in your flesh"
- LOGOS: "You shall not make any cuts on your body for the dead"
- PNEUMA: "Do not cut your body for the dead"
- Ruling: "a cutting for the dead [seret la-nefesh] you shall not make in your flesh" (EMET) — preserves the technical term with bracket; "in your flesh" (ba-vesarkhem) is the Hebrew's precise phrase; LOGOS/PNEUMA's "body" is a loose equivalent; LOGOS's "any cuts" pluralizes unnecessarily.

---

**D-LEV19-050 | V. 28 | tattoo rendering**
- EMET: "tattoo writing [ketovet qa'aqa'] you shall not put on yourselves"
- LOGOS: "or tattoo yourselves"
- PNEUMA: "or mark yourself with tattoos"
- Ruling: "and tattoo writing [ketovet qa'aqa'] you shall not put on yourselves" (EMET) — preserves the technical term with bracket; ketovet qa'aqa' is the only occurrence of qa'aqa' in the Hebrew Bible. "You shall not put on yourselves" preserves the Hebrew natan ("to put, place").

---

**D-LEV19-051 | V. 29 | "depravity" vs. "wickedness"**
- EMET/LOGOS: "depravity [zimmah]"
- PNEUMA: "wickedness"
- Ruling: "depravity [zimmah]" — zimmah is a specific term for moral/sexual licentiousness; "depravity" is more precise and the established project rendering. "Wickedness" (PNEUMA) is too generic.

---

**D-LEV19-052 | V. 31 | yid'onim rendering**
- EMET: "spirits of the dead [yid'onim]"
- LOGOS: "necromancers"
- PNEUMA: "spiritists"
- Ruling: "spirits of the dead [yid'onim]" (EMET) — yid'onim refers to the familiar spirits contacted through mediums (from yada, "to know"), not the practitioners themselves. "Necromancers" (LOGOS) conflates the spirit with the practitioner; "spiritists" (PNEUMA) is a modern term with imprecise reference.

---

**D-LEV19-053 | V. 31 | "defiled" vs. "make yourselves unclean"**
- EMET: "to be defiled by them"
- LOGOS: "make yourselves unclean by them"
- PNEUMA: "you will be defiled by them"
- Ruling: "to be defiled by them" (EMET) — the Hebrew is an infinitive construct le-tum'ah vohem expressing purpose/result; "to be defiled" preserves the infinitive form. LOGOS's active voice and PNEUMA's future indicative both alter the grammatical form.

---

**D-LEV19-054 | V. 32 | "gray head" / "elder" vs. "old man" vs. "the aged/elderly"**
- EMET: "Before a gray head [seva]... honor the face of an elder [zaqen]"
- LOGOS: "You shall stand up before the gray head... honor the face of an old man"
- PNEUMA: "Stand up in the presence of the aged... Show respect for the elderly"
- Ruling: "Before a gray head you shall rise, and you shall honor the face of an elder" — "gray head" (EMET/LOGOS) for seva is the concrete image; "elder" for zaqen is preferred over LOGOS's "old man" (loses the social-authority dimension of zaqen) and PNEUMA's "the elderly" (too generic). "Rise" for taqum is more precise than "stand up."

---

**D-LEV19-055 | V. 33 | "sojourner" vs. "foreigner...stranger" — CRITICAL RULING VIOLATION**
- EMET/LOGOS: "when a sojourner sojourns with you in your land"
- PNEUMA: "When a foreigner lives as a stranger in your land"
- Ruling: "when a sojourner sojourns with you in your land" — binding terminology ruling. PNEUMA uses both "foreigner" and "stranger" where the Hebrew has a single term ger ("sojourner"). Both substitutions are rejected.

---

**D-LEV19-056 | V. 33 | "oppress" vs. "wrong" vs. "mistreat"**
- EMET: "you shall not oppress [lo tonu] him"
- LOGOS: "you shall not wrong him"
- PNEUMA: "do not mistreat them"
- Ruling: "you shall not wrong him" (LOGOS) — yanah ("to oppress, mistreat, wrong") has a broad range; "wrong" captures it without over-specifying the type of harm. PNEUMA's "them" shifts incorrectly to plural.

---

**D-LEV19-057 | V. 34 | "sojourner" vs. "foreigner" — CRITICAL RULING VIOLATION (repeated)**
- EMET/LOGOS: "the sojourner who sojourns with you"
- PNEUMA: "The foreigner living among you"
- Ruling: "the sojourner who sojourns with you" — binding ruling. PNEUMA's substitution of "foreigner" for ger is rejected. The echo of ger/gerim at v. 34b ("for gerim were you in Egypt") is the theological pivot of the verse; "foreigner" destroys that verbal link.

---

**D-LEV19-058 | V. 34 | "native-born Israelite" vs. "native among you"**
- EMET/LOGOS: "as a native among you"
- PNEUMA: "treated the same as a native-born Israelite"
- Ruling: "as a native among you" — ezrach is "native" or "indigenous person"; "among you" is contextually understood to mean native Israelite, but spelling it out as "native-born Israelite" (PNEUMA) adds a specification the Hebrew does not contain.

---

**D-LEV19-059 | V. 34 | "you were sojourners" vs. "you were foreigners"**
- EMET/LOGOS: "for sojourners you were in the land of Egypt"
- PNEUMA: "for you were foreigners in Egypt"
- Ruling: "for sojourners you were in the land of Egypt" (EMET/LOGOS) — gerim is the Hebrew noun used here, echoing ger throughout the passage; "sojourners" preserves this critical verbal link. PNEUMA's "foreigners" and omission of "the land of" both weaken the verse's theological argument.

---

**D-LEV19-060 | V. 35 | "in measure, weight, or quantity" vs. variants**
- EMET: "in measure [ba-midah], in weight [ba-mishqal], or in liquid measure [ba-meshurah]"
- LOGOS: "in measures of length or weight or quantity"
- PNEUMA: "in measuring length, weight, or quantity"
- Ruling: "in measure, in weight, or in quantity" — midah covers all dimensions of measure (not only length as LOGOS/PNEUMA specify); meshurah is a liquid measure, but "quantity" is an acceptable functional equivalent in English for the third term.

---

**D-LEV19-061 | V. 36 | "righteousness" vs. "just" vs. "honest" for tsedeq in measures**
- EMET: "Balances of righteousness [mozne tsedeq], stones of righteousness [avne tsedeq], an ephah of righteousness [eifat tsedeq], and a hin of righteousness [hin tsedeq]"
- LOGOS: "just balances, just weights, a just ephah, and a just hin"
- PNEUMA: "honest scales, honest weights, and honest measures — dry and liquid alike"
- Ruling: "Balances of righteousness [mozne tsedeq], weights of righteousness [avne tsedeq], an ephah of righteousness [eifat tsedeq], and a hin of righteousness [hin tsedeq]" (EMET, with "weights" for avne) — the fourfold repetition of tsedeq ("righteousness") is a deliberate cross-chapter verbal link to vv. 15 and 35. "Just" (LOGOS) and "honest" (PNEUMA) both flatten tsedeq to a single modifier and destroy the anaphoric structure. "Stones" (EMET, literal Hebrew avne) is replaced by "weights" for clarity without losing the meaning. PNEUMA's omission of the ephah/hin distinction ("dry and liquid alike") loses the two specific terms.

---

**D-LEV19-062 | V. 37 | "judgments" vs. "rules" vs. "ordinances" for mishpatai**
- EMET: "all my statutes and all my judgments"
- LOGOS: "all my statutes and all my rules"
- PNEUMA: "all my statutes and all my ordinances"
- Ruling: "all my statutes and all my ordinances" (PNEUMA) — mishpatim is the established project rendering as "ordinances" (cf. Lev 18 consensus and project memory), which is broader and less juridically specific than "judgments" (EMET) and more dignified than "rules" (LOGOS).
